top5Opportunities.statement: Minimize the time to detect a glycol jacket blockage or partial restriction before fermenter temperature deviates >0.5°C from setpoint
Minimize the likelihood of accepting a false-normal flow reading that masks a partially blocked glycol jacket
Minimize the likelihood of discovering a fermentation temperature excursion only after the 2–4 week fermentation cycle has completed, when correction is no longer possible
Minimize batch-to-batch variation in fermentation temperature profile caused by uneven glycol distribution across cooling zones (target: <±0.5°C)
Minimize the number of undetected glycol pump degradation events that reduce per-zone cooling capacity by >15%

needs.statement: Minimize the likelihood of undetected calibration drift exceeding ±0.5% of reading over a 12-month service period
needs.importanceRationale: Brewery glycol system accuracy directly determines fermenter temperature control precision. A ±0.5% flow drift changes cooling capacity delivery by up to 2°C at low flow rates, which is the difference between on-spec and off-spec fermentation for lager and wheat beer styles with tight temperature windows (1–2°C tolerance).
needs.satisfactionRationale: PW drifts 2–5% over life (dominant 50–60%); mag stable but minority; manual = zero measurement

needs.statement: Minimize variation in flow measurement accuracy caused by glycol concentration fluctuations between 25% and 40% propylene glycol
needs.importanceRationale: Brewery glycol systems are routinely topped up with water or undiluted glycol rather than pre-mixed solution, causing concentration drift between seasonal service intervals. Speed-of-sound in propylene glycol changes ~2 m/s per 1% concentration, so a 15% concentration shift creates 30 m/s bias that translates to 5–10% flow measurement error without active compensation.
needs.satisfactionRationale: No incumbent compensates for glycol concentration changes

needs.statement: Increase the ability to measure flow rate across the full operating range (0.5–50 L/min) without sensor reconfiguration
needs.importanceRationale: Brewery glycol systems serve vessels ranging from 100L pilot tanks to 5,000L fermenters on the same distribution manifold. Per-zone flow rates span 0.5 L/min at crash-cool maintenance through 50 L/min during peak summer fermentation demand; a 100:1 turndown sensor eliminates the need to select different sensors for different vessel sizes.
needs.satisfactionRationale: Mag handles full range (100:1 turndown); PW limited but functional

needs.statement: Minimize the likelihood of false flow readings caused by air bubble entrainment during glycol system refill or maintenance events
needs.importanceRationale: Glycol refill events introduce air slugs that cause paddle-wheel sensors to jam or stall, reporting either zero flow or inflated readings for 5–30 minutes. During active fermentation this false reading can trigger premature cooling shutoff, allowing the fermenter temperature to rise during a critical fermentation phase.
needs.satisfactionRationale: PW jams/stalls with air; mag less affected but still errors

needs.statement: Minimize the time to detect a glycol jacket blockage or partial restriction before fermenter temperature deviates >0.5°C from setpoint
needs.importanceRationale: A glycol jacket blockage is invisible to the operator until the fermenter temperature probe reacts — typically 30–60 minutes after the flow fault, by which time the fermentation excursion may be irreversible. Real-time per-zone flow monitoring detects the blockage within seconds, enabling glycol circuit intervention before any thermal excursion occurs.
needs.satisfactionRationale: No incumbent provides glycol jacket flow monitoring; manual inspection only

needs.statement: Minimize the number of undetected glycol pump degradation events that reduce per-zone cooling capacity by >15%
needs.importanceRationale: Glycol pump impeller cavitation and bearing wear reduce total system flow by 15–30% over 12–18 months before visible failure. Without flow trending across zones, the degradation is invisible until either the pump fails completely or a temperature excursion reveals reduced cooling capacity — at which point recovery requires emergency service during active fermentation.
needs.satisfactionRationale: No incumbent trends pump degradation; manual checks only

needs.statement: Increase the ability to distinguish genuine process flow deviations from sensor measurement noise with >95% classification accuracy
needs.importanceRationale: Paddle-wheel sensors in glycol service exhibit high noise-to-signal ratios due to bearing friction variation. If the system cannot distinguish noise from a genuine 15% flow deviation, it either generates constant false alarms that operators learn to ignore, or it misses real blockages — both outcomes undermine the case for flow monitoring entirely.
needs.satisfactionRationale: PW has poor S/N (dominant); mag better but no classification logic

needs.statement: Minimize the time to detect a glycol leak in the distribution system before coolant volume loss exceeds 5% of reservoir capacity
needs.importanceRationale: Food-grade propylene glycol costs $3–$6 per litre; a 5% reservoir loss on a 500L system represents $75–$150 in coolant waste per undetected leak event. Beyond cost, propylene glycol contact with product can trigger FDA/TTB contamination reporting, forcing batch dumping if the leak source is traced to a fermenter jacket.
needs.satisfactionRationale: No automated leak detection in incumbent brewery glycol setups

needs.statement: Increase the ability to determine the correct glycol flow rate setpoint within ±10% of actual cooling load for each fermenter size and beer style
needs.importanceRationale: Brewery process engineers must configure glycol flow setpoints before a fermentation batch begins. Without measured flow data from previous batches of the same beer style and vessel size, setpoints are based on rule-of-thumb estimates that are often 20–40% over-specified, wasting chiller energy during periods of low fermentation exotherm.
needs.satisfactionRationale: Knowledge/engineering need; sensor-independent

needs.statement: Increase the ability to calculate required glycol flow rates for different beer styles and fermenter sizes from a single sensor configuration
needs.importanceRationale: Multi-vessel breweries produce 5–15 different beer styles per season, each with different fermentation heat curves and temperature profiles. A sensor platform that integrates with brewery management software to recommend per-style glycol flow setpoints reduces the setup expertise required and allows junior cellar staff to configure fermentation cooling without specialist knowledge.
needs.satisfactionRationale: Knowledge need; sensor-independent

needs.statement: Minimize the time required to install and commission a flow sensor on an existing glycol line without draining the system
needs.importanceRationale: Brewery glycol systems are in continuous use with active fermentations that cannot be interrupted for sensor installation. Draining the glycol system requires postponing all ongoing fermentations and a 2–4 hour system shutdown; a clamp-on or hot-tap installation that avoids system drain eliminates this operational constraint entirely.
needs.satisfactionRationale: All inline sensors require system drain for installation

needs.statement: Minimize the likelihood of systematic measurement error >1% caused by incorrect fluid-type configuration at commissioning
needs.importanceRationale: Ultrasonic sensor commissioning requires correct fluid-type configuration for the specific glycol concentration in use. Brewery maintenance staff performing first-time sensor installations regularly set the wrong concentration value, introducing a systematic 1–5% bias that persists undetected for months because no reference measurement is available for comparison.
needs.satisfactionRationale: PW needs no fluid config (zero error risk); mag minimal config

needs.statement: Increase the ability to verify sensor accuracy within 5 minutes before starting a fermentation batch
needs.importanceRationale: Brewers pitch yeast and start fermentation on a fixed schedule; pre-batch verification of sensor accuracy is a quality checkpoint that most small breweries skip because current sensors have no built-in self-check. A 5-minute automated accuracy confirmation before each batch prevents the temperature excursion scenario where a faulty sensor goes undetected until the batch is beyond recovery.
needs.satisfactionRationale: PW has no self-diagnostics; mag basic only; mostly skipped in practice

needs.statement: Minimize the likelihood of accepting a false-normal flow reading that masks a partially blocked glycol jacket
needs.importanceRationale: Scale and debris deposits in fermenter glycol jackets restrict flow by 20–60% before the blockage becomes detectable via temperature alone. The dangerous scenario is partial blockage — where flow appears adequate but cooling capacity is reduced — because no incumbent sensor platform flags this condition, allowing full fermentation cycles to complete with degraded cooling.
needs.satisfactionRationale: No incumbent detects partial jacket blockage — completely unaddressed

needs.statement: Increase the ability to isolate flow anomaly root cause (sensor vs glycol pump vs zone valve) within 5 minutes
needs.importanceRationale: When a flow anomaly is detected in a brewery glycol system, the cellarman must determine whether the fault is in the sensor, the pump, or a zone valve before taking corrective action. Without diagnostic data from the sensor, root cause isolation requires sequential physical inspection of each component — a process taking 30–90 minutes that extends the fermentation risk window.
needs.satisfactionRationale: No incumbent provides diagnostic data for root cause isolation

needs.statement: Minimize the operator expertise required to interpret flow anomaly data and determine the correct corrective action
needs.importanceRationale: Small and mid-size breweries (<5,000 HL/year) typically employ cellarmen with practical training rather than engineering degrees. Complex flow sensor outputs requiring interpretation of signal quality metrics or oscilloscope data are inaccessible to this audience; plain-language fault alerts with actionable guidance reduce the expertise threshold to near-zero.
needs.satisfactionRationale: PW: no diagnostics; mag: complex outputs requiring training

needs.statement: Increase the sensor service life achievable without scheduled maintenance interventions affecting measurement accuracy (target: >3 years)
needs.importanceRationale: Paddle-wheel sensors in glycol service require bearing replacement every 6–18 months due to propylene glycol's mild abrasive properties and low lubricity. Each replacement requires system drain, sensor removal, rebuild, and recommissioning at a labour cost of $150–$300 per point; a 3-year maintenance-free service life halves the total ownership cost.
needs.satisfactionRationale: PW bearing life 6–18 mo in glycol dominates; mag >10yr but minority

needs.statement: Minimize the total installed cost per glycol flow measurement point including sensor, wiring, and power supply (target: <$500)
needs.importanceRationale: A typical 10-vessel brewery requires 10–20 measurement points on glycol distribution circuits. At $800–$2,000 per point for magnetic flowmeters, full instrumentation costs $8,000–$40,000 — prohibitive for small breweries with $50K–$200K total capital budgets. A $500 per point total installed cost opens the monitoring market to the 80% of breweries currently using no flow measurement.
needs.satisfactionRationale: PW at $50–150 well under target; mag at $800–2,000 exceeds it

needs.statement: Minimize the likelihood of sensor-induced flow path contamination requiring line flushing after sensor installation or replacement
needs.importanceRationale: Brewery glycol systems can carry residual contamination from sensor materials (lubricants, thread sealants, O-ring compounds) that require full system flushing if they migrate into the circuit. Flushing a glycol circuit takes 2–4 hours and wastes 10–20L of propylene glycol; sensors with food-contact-rated materials and clean installation designs eliminate this requirement.
needs.satisfactionRationale: PW: some debris risk; mag: clean. Mixed

needs.statement: Minimize the permanent pressure drop across the measurement point that increases glycol pump energy consumption
needs.importanceRationale: Brewery glycol pumps typically run 16–24 hours per day during peak fermentation season. Sensors with high pressure drop (>100 mbar) at design flow rates increase pump energy consumption by 5–15% annually; in a 20-vessel brewery running 200L/min total glycol flow, this represents hundreds of dollars in annual pump operating cost per sensor.
needs.satisfactionRationale: PW/mag: minimal drop; orifice: high. Mixed

needs.statement: Minimize batch-to-batch variation in fermentation temperature profile caused by uneven glycol distribution across cooling zones (target: <±0.5°C)
needs.importanceRationale: Fermentation temperature consistency determines ester and fusel alcohol profiles that define a beer's flavour character. Batch-to-batch temperature variation >±0.5°C at the same fermentation stage produces noticeable flavour inconsistency; for contract and craft brewers, this variation directly undermines their product quality claims and can lead to customer complaints and returns.
needs.satisfactionRationale: Without per-zone flow monitoring, glycol distribution is uncontrolled

needs.statement: Increase the ability to cool multiple fermenters simultaneously without flow starvation to any individual cooling zone
needs.importanceRationale: Multi-vessel brewery glycol systems share a single chiller across 5–20 fermenters. When multiple fermenters enter active fermentation simultaneously during warm months, competition for glycol flow creates starvation in zones furthest from the pump. Per-zone flow measurement enables the chiller controller to dynamically redistribute flow before temperature excursions develop.
needs.satisfactionRationale: No per-zone flow visibility → starvation undetected until temp drifts

needs.statement: Increase the ability to present quantitative cooling system performance data to brewery ownership and quality assurance teams
needs.importanceRationale: Brewery QA certification schemes (e.g., FSSC 22000, BRC) require documented evidence of fermentation temperature control. Quantitative glycol flow data provides the cooling system performance record that supports temperature control documentation, enabling breweries to pass certification audits with data-driven evidence rather than manual logbooks.
needs.satisfactionRationale: PW: basic pulse only; mag: better data but too expensive to deploy at every point

needs.statement: Minimize the likelihood of discovering a fermentation temperature excursion only after the 2–4 week fermentation cycle has completed, when correction is no longer possible
needs.importanceRationale: A batch of lager or ale worth $3,000–$30,000 can be ruined by a 24-hour temperature excursion during the first week of fermentation. The current detection paradigm relies on fermenter temperature probes that respond 30–60 minutes after the glycol flow fault — after the yeast biology has already been affected. Flow-based early warning cuts the detection lag to under 2 minutes.
needs.satisfactionRationale: No flow-based early warning exists; relies on slow-response fermenter temp probe

needs.statement: Minimize the likelihood of false flow readings when air entrainment exceeds 2% volume during glycol system refill or valve-switching events
needs.importanceRationale: Zone valve switching and glycol system top-up are routine brewery operations performed multiple times daily. Air slugs introduced during these operations cause ultrasonic transit-time errors and paddle-wheel stalls that create false low-flow alarms. Sensors without air-tolerance logic generate unnecessary alert fatigue that degrades operator response to genuine alarms.

needs.statement: Minimize the time to recover valid flow measurement after transient air entrainment events dissipate from the glycol circuit
needs.importanceRationale: If a sensor takes 5–15 minutes to recover valid measurement after an air entrainment event, any cooling fault that occurs during this blind window goes undetected. Quick recovery (under 30 seconds) ensures the monitoring system resumes protective function almost immediately after routine maintenance operations complete.

needs.statement: Minimize the measurement accuracy degradation from insufficient straight pipe run in space-constrained brewery glycol manifolds with elbows and T-junctions within 5–10 DN upstream
needs.importanceRationale: Brewery cellar glycol manifolds are routed to fit around tanks, walls, and walkways, with sensor positions determined by available pipe runs rather than sensor specifications. The typical available straight run is 2–3×DN rather than the 5–10×DN required; sensors tolerant of disturbed profiles can be placed where needed rather than where the geometry is ideal.

needs.statement: Minimize the duration of measurement blackout caused by partial pipe drainage during glycol circuit maintenance
needs.importanceRationale: Glycol circuit maintenance tasks (filter changes, valve replacements, sensor swaps) partially drain pipe sections upstream of the sensor. Sensors that take 10–30 minutes to re-establish valid measurement after partial refill create monitoring blind spots that coincide with the disturbance period when faults are most likely to be introduced.

needs.statement: Minimize the transient measurement error during crash-cooling sequences where glycol temperature changes exceed the compensation algorithm response rate at >2°C/min
needs.importanceRationale: Crash cooling reduces fermenter temperature from 20°C to 2°C at rates of 1–3°C/hour by maximising glycol flow and reducing glycol inlet temperature. During this phase the glycol temperature changes rapidly; sensors whose compensation algorithms lag the temperature change produce transient flow over-reads that can mislead the controller into reducing pump speed prematurely.

needs.statement: Minimize the likelihood of signal-to-noise degradation below the transit-time detection threshold during low-demand glycol flow in small cooling zones
needs.importanceRationale: Small fermenters (100–300L) in nano and pilot brewery setups use DN12–DN15 pipework with glycol flows as low as 0.3–1.5 L/min during conditioning phases. Transit-time sensors lose signal-to-noise ratio at these low velocities; sensors with insufficient sensitivity to low-demand zones leave the most critical small-batch fermentations unmonitored.

needs.statement: Minimize the risk of acoustic coupling failure when glycol-to-water ratio shifts outside the validated acoustic impedance range due to uncontrolled top-up or evaporation
needs.importanceRationale: Brewery glycol reservoirs lose water by evaporation in warm cellars, gradually increasing glycol concentration beyond the sensor's calibrated range. This acoustic coupling shift produces progressive measurement drift that is invisible without a refractometer check. Sensors with wide validated acoustic impedance range or auto-detecting glycol concentration tolerate this drift without recalibration.
